annotations.label: Arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Arithmetic is the fundamental branch of mathematics that studies numbers and their operations

annotations.note: A meta-description of a branch of mathematics, not a formalizable mathematical object.

annotations.label: Number theory

anchor.snippet: number theory is restricted to the study of integers

annotations.note: Describes a field of study rather than a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Numbers

anchor.snippet: Numbers are mathematical objects used to count quantities and measure magnitudes

annotations.note: Mathlib has no single generic 'number' type; numbers are formalized as specific types like ℕ, ℤ, ℝ.

annotations.label: Natural numbers

anchor.snippet: The natural numbers are whole numbers that start from 1 and go to infinity

annotations.note: The type ℕ (Nat) is a Lean core inductive type used throughout Mathlib, though Mathlib's ℕ includes 0 unlike this article's convention.

annotations.label: Whole numbers

anchor.snippet: The whole numbers are identical to the natural numbers

annotations.note: Identical to the natural number type ℕ (Nat), a Lean core type.

annotations.label: Integers

anchor.snippet: The set of integers encompasses both positive and negative whole numbers

annotations.note: The type ℤ (Int) is a Lean core type whose commutative ring structure is developed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cardinal numbers

anchor.snippet: Cardinal numbers, like one, two, and three, are numbers that express the quantity of objects

annotations.note: Cardinal is defined as the quotient of types by equinumerosity, capturing quantity/size.

annotations.label: Ordinal numbers

anchor.snippet: Ordinal numbers, such as first, second, and third, indicate order or placement in a series

annotations.note: Ordinal is defined as isomorphism classes of well-ordered types.

annotations.label: Rational number

anchor.snippet: A number is rational if it can be represented as the ratio of two integers

annotations.note: The type ℚ (Rat) is a Lean core structure with numerator and denominator fields.

annotations.label: Decimal fractions

anchor.snippet: Decimal fractions like 0.3 and 25.12 are a special type of rational numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no distinguished subtype of rationals with denominators that are powers of 10.

annotations.label: Rational numbers and decimals

anchor.snippet: Every rational number corresponds to a finite or a repeating decimal

annotations.note: No theorem characterizing rationals as eventually-periodic decimal expansions was found.

annotations.label: Irrational numbers

anchor.snippet: Irrational numbers are numbers that cannot be expressed through the ratio of two integers

annotations.note: Irrational x is defined as x not lying in the range of the rational-to-real coercion.

annotations.label: Real numbers

anchor.snippet: makes up the set of real numbers

annotations.note: Real is the type ℝ, constructed as a quotient of Cauchy sequences of rationals.

annotations.label: Numeral

anchor.snippet: A numeral is a symbol to represent a number

annotations.note: Symbolic representation of numbers is not formalized as a mathematical object.

annotations.label: Non-positional numeral system

anchor.snippet: the value of a digit does not depend on its position in the numeral

annotations.note: Numeral-system notation conventions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unary numeral system

anchor.snippet: The simplest non-positional system is the unary numeral system

annotations.note: Though Nat's zero/succ structure is essentially unary, the unary numeral system itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Egyptian hieroglyphic numerals

anchor.snippet: Egyptian hieroglyphics had a more complex non-positional numeral system

annotations.note: Historical numeral systems are not formalized.

annotations.label: Positional numeral system

anchor.snippet: A numeral system is positional if the position of a basic numeral in a compound expression determines its value

annotations.note: Nat.digits b n gives the positional base-b digit representation, capturing positional value but not the notion of a 'numeral system' abstractly.

annotations.label: Decimal system

anchor.snippet: In the common decimal system, also called the Hindu–Arabic numeral system

annotations.note: Nat.digits 10 n produces the base-10 positional representation.

annotations.label: Binary system

anchor.snippet: used extensively in computer arithmetic is the binary system

annotations.note: Nat.digits 2 n produces the base-2 positional representation.

annotations.label: Arithmetic operations

anchor.snippet: Arithmetic operations are ways of combining, transforming, or manipulating numbers

annotations.note: A general informal umbrella term, not a single formalizable object.

annotations.label: Identity element

anchor.snippet: The identity element or neutral element of an operation does not cause any change

annotations.note: Captured by MulOneClass/AddZeroClass via one_mul/mul_one (and additive analogues).

annotations.label: Inverse element

anchor.snippet: The inverse element is the element that results in the identity element when combined with another element

annotations.note: In a group, mul_inv_cancel states a * a⁻¹ = 1, expressing the inverse element giving the identity.

annotations.label: Inverse operation

anchor.snippet: one operation is the inverse of another operation if it undoes the first operation

annotations.note: No general 'inverse operation' relation is formalized, though specific instances like sub_eq_add_neg exist.

annotations.label: Commutativity

anchor.snippet: An operation is commutative if the order of the arguments can be changed without affecting the results

annotations.note: CommMagma (and the core Std.Commutative class) axiomatizes a commutative binary operation.

annotations.label: Associativity

anchor.snippet: An operation is associative if, in a series of two operations, it does not matter which operation is carried out first

annotations.note: Semigroup (and the core Std.Associative class) axiomatizes an associative binary operation via mul_assoc.

annotations.label: Addition

anchor.snippet: Addition is an arithmetic operation in which two numbers, called the addends, are combined into a single number, called the sum

annotations.note: The Add/HAdd operation is a Lean core typeclass whose structures (AddCommMonoid, etc.) are developed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Summation

anchor.snippet: The term summation is used if several additions are performed in a row

annotations.note: Finset.sum (notation ∑) sums a function over a finite set, i.e. repeated addition.

annotations.label: Counting

anchor.snippet: Counting is a type of repeated addition in which the number 1 is continuously added

annotations.note: Repeated addition of 1 is realized by the core successor function Nat.succ, but 'counting' itself is not a formalized notion.

annotations.label: Subtraction

anchor.snippet: Subtraction is the inverse of addition

annotations.note: sub_eq_add_neg expresses subtraction as adding the additive inverse, i.e. the inverse of addition.

annotations.label: Additive identity and inverse

anchor.snippet: The additive identity element is 0 and the additive inverse of a number is the negative of that number

annotations.note: add_zero gives 0 as additive identity and neg_add_cancel gives the negative as additive inverse.

annotations.label: Addition is commutative and associative

anchor.snippet: Addition is both commutative and associative

annotations.note: add_comm and add_assoc hold in any additive commutative monoid (to_additive duals of mul_comm/mul_assoc).

annotations.label: Multiplication

anchor.snippet: Multiplication is an arithmetic operation in which two numbers, called the multiplier and the multiplicand, are combined into a single number called the product

annotations.note: The Mul/HMul operation is a Lean core typeclass whose structures (Monoid, etc.) are developed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Division

anchor.snippet: Division is the inverse of multiplication

annotations.note: div_eq_mul_inv states a / b = a * b⁻¹, expressing division as the inverse of multiplication.

annotations.label: Multiplicative identity and inverse

anchor.snippet: The multiplicative identity element is 1 and the multiplicative inverse of a number is the reciprocal of that number

annotations.note: mul_one gives 1 as multiplicative identity and mul_inv_cancel gives the reciprocal as multiplicative inverse.

annotations.label: Multiplication is commutative and associative

anchor.snippet: Multiplication is both commutative and associative

annotations.note: mul_comm and mul_assoc hold in any commutative monoid.

annotations.label: Exponentiation

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation is an arithmetic operation in which a number, known as the base, is raised to the power of another number, known as the exponent

annotations.note: Monoid.npow (with HPow notation ^) defines raising a base to a natural-number power.

annotations.label: Roots

anchor.snippet: Roots are a special type of exponentiation using a fractional exponent

annotations.note: Real.rpow allows real (hence fractional) exponents, so nth roots are x ^ (1/n).

annotations.label: Logarithm

anchor.snippet: Logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation

annotations.note: Real.log is the natural logarithm (with Real.logb for arbitrary base), the inverse of exp/rpow.

annotations.label: Exponentiation/logarithm identity elements

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation and logarithm do not have general identity elements and inverse elements

annotations.note: A meta-observation about absence of structure, not a formalized theorem.

annotations.label: Exponentiation/logarithm not commutative or associative

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation and logarithm are neither commutative nor associative

annotations.note: No dedicated theorem stating non-commutativity/non-associativity of these operations was found.

annotations.label: Integer arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Integer arithmetic is the branch of arithmetic that deals with the manipulation of positive and negative whole numbers

annotations.note: The integers ℤ and their commutative-ring operations are fully formalized, though 'integer arithmetic' as a branch is meta.

annotations.label: Addition with carries

anchor.snippet: in the method addition with carries , the two numbers are written one above the other

annotations.note: A pen-and-paper algorithm, not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Multiplication as repeated addition

anchor.snippet: A basic technique of integer multiplication employs repeated addition

annotations.note: nsmul_eq_mul states n • a = n * a, equating repeated addition (n•a) with multiplication.

annotations.label: Long multiplication

anchor.snippet: A common technique for multiplication with larger numbers is called long multiplication

annotations.note: A computational algorithm, not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Long division

anchor.snippet: A common technique used for division is called long division

annotations.note: A computational algorithm; only Euclidean division (Nat.div) is formalized, not the long-division method.

annotations.label: Integers not closed under division

anchor.snippet: Integer arithmetic is not closed under division

annotations.note: The non-closure of ℤ under field division is not stated as a theorem (Mathlib uses truncating Int division).

annotations.label: Exponentiation by squaring

anchor.snippet: A more efficient technique used for large exponents is exponentiation by squaring

annotations.note: npowBinRec computes powers by binary recursion, i.e. exponentiation by squaring.

annotations.label: Integers not closed under logarithm/exponentiation

anchor.snippet: Integer arithmetic is not closed under logarithm and under exponentiation with negative exponents

annotations.note: Non-closure of ℤ under log/negative exponents is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Number theory

anchor.snippet: Number theory studies the structure and properties of integers as well as the relations and laws between them

annotations.note: Describes a field of study, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: every integer greater than 1 is either a prime number or can be represented as a unique product of prime numbers

annotations.note: Explicitly labeled the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, proving uniqueness of prime factorization.

annotations.label: Euclid's theorem

anchor.snippet: Euclid's theorem states that there are infinitely many prime numbers

annotations.note: States that for every n there is a prime p ≥ n, i.e. infinitely many primes.

annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem

anchor.snippet: Fermat's Last Theorem is the statement that no positive integer values exist for

annotations.note: The statement of FLT is defined (proof not in Mathlib), with special cases like n=3 proven.

annotations.label: Rational number arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Rational number arithmetic is the branch of arithmetic that deals with the manipulation of numbers that can be expressed as a ratio of two integers

annotations.note: ℚ and its field operations are fully formalized, though 'rational arithmetic' as a branch is meta.

annotations.label: Adding rationals with common denominator

anchor.snippet: If two rational numbers have the same denominator then they can be added by adding their numerators and keeping the common denominator

annotations.note: div_add_div_same states a/c + b/c = (a+b)/c in any division ring.

annotations.label: Multiplying rationals

anchor.snippet: Two rational numbers are multiplied by multiplying their numerators and their denominators respectively

annotations.note: div_mul_div_comm states a/b * (c/d) = a*c/(b*d).

annotations.label: Dividing rationals

anchor.snippet: Dividing one rational number by another can be achieved by multiplying the first number with the reciprocal of the second number

annotations.note: div_eq_mul_inv states a / b = a * b⁻¹, i.e. multiply by the reciprocal.

annotations.label: Rationals closed under division

anchor.snippet: rational number arithmetic is closed under division as long as the divisor is not 0

annotations.note: ℚ being a Field makes division total (with a/0 = 0 convention), but closure-except-0 is not stated as a separate theorem.

annotations.label: Integers/rationals not closed under exp/log

anchor.snippet: Both integer arithmetic and rational number arithmetic are not closed under exponentiation and logarithm

annotations.note: Non-closure of ℤ and ℚ under exp/log is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Decimal fraction notation

anchor.snippet: The decimal fraction notation is a special way of representing rational numbers whose denominator is a power of 10

annotations.note: Decimal-fraction notation is not formalized as a distinct object.

annotations.label: Not all rationals have finite decimals

anchor.snippet: Not all rational numbers have a finite representation in the decimal notation

annotations.note: No theorem about finiteness of decimal representations of rationals was found.

annotations.label: Repeating decimals are rational

anchor.snippet: Every repeating decimal expresses a rational number

annotations.note: No theorem stating that repeating decimals are rational was found.

annotations.label: Real number arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Real number arithmetic is the branch of arithmetic that deals with the manipulation of both rational and irrational numbers

annotations.note: ℝ and its complete linearly-ordered field operations are formalized, though 'real arithmetic' as a branch is meta.

annotations.label: Reals closed under exponentiation

anchor.snippet: real number arithmetic is closed under exponentiation as long as it uses a positive number as its base

annotations.note: rpow_pos_of_pos shows that a positive real base raised to any real exponent yields a positive real.

annotations.label: Truncation

anchor.snippet: a certain number of leftmost digits are kept and remaining digits are discarded or replaced by zeros

annotations.note: Digit truncation is not formalized as such (only floor/ceil-style operations exist).

annotations.label: Rounding

anchor.snippet: Rounding is a similar process in which the last preserved digit is increased by one

annotations.note: round maps an element to the nearest integer, i.e. rounding.

annotations.label: Significant digits

anchor.snippet: round each measurement to a certain number of digits, called significant digits

annotations.note: The notion of significant digits/measurement precision is not formalized.

annotations.label: Uncertainty propagation (addition/subtraction)

anchor.snippet: When adding or subtracting two or more quantities, add the absolute uncertainties of each summand together

annotations.note: Error-propagation rules for measurements are not formalized.

annotations.label: Uncertainty propagation (multiplication/division)

anchor.snippet: When multiplying or dividing two or more quantities, add the relative uncertainties of each factor together

annotations.note: Error-propagation rules for measurements are not formalized.

annotations.label: Interval arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Interval arithmetic describes operations on intervals

annotations.note: No interval-arithmetic operations were found (intervals like Set.Icc exist but not arithmetic on them).

annotations.label: Affine form

anchor.snippet: An affine form is a number together with error terms that describe how the number may deviate from the actual magnitude

annotations.note: Affine arithmetic / affine forms are not formalized.

annotations.label: Scientific notation

anchor.snippet: a number is decomposed into the product of a number between 1 and 10, called the significand

annotations.note: The core OfScientific class (with Mathlib's LawfulOfScientific) represents significand×base^exponent literals, but not a general scientific-notation theory.

annotations.label: Floating-point arithmetic

anchor.snippet: It represents real numbers similar to the scientific notation through three numbers: a significand, a base, and an exponent

annotations.note: Mathlib does not develop a theory of floating-point arithmetic (core has a Float type only).

annotations.label: Floating-point addition not associative

anchor.snippet: floating-point addition is not associative

annotations.note: No theorem on non-associativity of floating-point addition was found.

annotations.label: Mental arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Mental arithmetic relies exclusively on the mind without external tools

annotations.note: An informal human practice, not a mathematical object.

annotations.label: Compensation method

anchor.snippet: One such technique is the compensation method, which consists in altering the numbers to make the calculation easier

annotations.note: A mental-calculation heuristic, not formalized.

annotations.label: Modular arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Modular arithmetic operates on a finite set of numbers

annotations.note: ZMod n is the ring of integers modulo n, with full modular arithmetic.

annotations.label: Vector and matrix arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Vector arithmetic and matrix arithmetic describe arithmetic operations on vectors and matrices

annotations.note: Matrix m n α with its addition/multiplication formalizes matrix arithmetic (vectors as Fin n → α).

annotations.label: Decimal arithmetic

anchor.snippet: decimal arithmetic describes arithmetic operations in the decimal system

annotations.note: Arithmetic performed specifically in base-10 representation is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Compound unit arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Compound unit arithmetic describes arithmetic operations performed on magnitudes with compound units

annotations.note: Arithmetic on quantities with units is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-Diophantine arithmetics

anchor.snippet: Non-Diophantine arithmetics are arithmetic systems that violate traditional arithmetic intuitions

annotations.note: Non-Diophantine arithmetic systems are not formalized.

annotations.label: Axiomatic foundations

anchor.snippet: Axiomatic foundations of arithmetic try to provide a small set of laws, called axioms

annotations.note: A meta-description of axiomatization, not itself a formalizable object.

annotations.label: Dedekind–Peano axioms

anchor.snippet: The Dedekind–Peano axioms provide an axiomatization of the arithmetic of natural numbers

annotations.note: The inductive type Nat realizes the Peano axioms, though there is no separate explicit axiom-set structure.

annotations.label: Peano axiom 1

anchor.snippet: 0 is a natural number.

annotations.note: Nat.zero : ℕ is a constructor of the core inductive type Nat.

annotations.label: Peano axiom 2 (successor)

anchor.snippet: For every natural number, there is a successor, which is also a natural number.

annotations.note: Nat.succ : ℕ → ℕ is the successor constructor of the core type Nat.

annotations.label: Peano axiom 3 (injectivity)

anchor.snippet: The successors of two different natural numbers are never identical.

annotations.note: Nat.succ_injective (a Lean core lemma) states the successor function is injective.

annotations.label: Peano axiom 4 (0 not a successor)

anchor.snippet: 0 is not the successor of a natural number.

annotations.note: Nat.succ_ne_zero (a Lean core lemma) states succ n ≠ 0.

annotations.label: Peano axiom 5 (induction)

anchor.snippet: If a set contains 0 and every successor then it contains every natural number.

annotations.note: Nat.rec is the core induction/recursion principle for ℕ.

annotations.label: Natural numbers as sets

anchor.snippet: Each natural number is represented by a unique set

annotations.note: Mathlib defines ℕ as an inductive type, not via the von Neumann set construction.

annotations.label: Integers as ordered pairs

anchor.snippet: Integers can be defined as ordered pairs of natural numbers where the second number is subtracted from the first one

annotations.note: Mathlib/core defines ℤ as an inductive type (ofNat/negSucc), not as a quotient of pairs of naturals.

annotations.label: Rational numbers as pairs

anchor.snippet: Rational numbers are defined as pairs of integers where the first number represents the numerator and the second number represents the denominator

annotations.note: The core Rat type is precisely a structure with a numerator (ℤ) and denominator (ℕ) in lowest terms.

annotations.label: Real numbers via Dedekind cuts

anchor.snippet: One way to construct the real numbers relies on the concept of Dedekind cuts

annotations.note: Mathlib defines DedekindCut as a Dedekind-completion construction (with DedekindCut ℚ ≃o EReal), though its canonical ℝ is built via Cauchy sequences.
